ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for hot dog bun as a composite food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hot dog bun serving is approximately 180 g, comprising a white bread bun filled with a chicken or beef sausage and topped with meat sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Hot dog bun with sausage — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery platform source; these have been retained as indicative values but downgraded to confidence 0.70 and flagged. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients are not reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: White bread GI reference — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite hot dog bun with sausage and meat sauce. GI of 72 is estimated from the white bread bun component, which is the dominant carbohydrate source. White bread GI = 70–75 (mean 72) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The sausage and meat sauce components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 37.2) / 100 = 26.8, rounded to 26.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 593 mg/100 g from processed sausage and condiments; saturated fat 5.56 g/100 g contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid hot dog buns due to the high sodium content (593 mg/100 g, equivalent to ~1,067 mg per 180 g serving), which approaches or exceeds single-meal sodium targets; request minimal condiments and consider a lower-sodium sausage alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white bread bun (GI ~72) combined with saturated fat (5.56 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.28 g/100 g) that worsen insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit hot dog buns because the refined white bread bun creates a high glycaemic load (~26.7 per serving), and the saturated and trans fat content from the processed sausage further impairs insulin sensitivity; substituting a wholegrain bun and requesting no added condiments can partially mitigate glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Trans fat 0.28 g/100 g and saturated fat 5.56 g/100 g from processed sausage; high sodium 593 mg/100 g; cholesterol 33.33 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid hot dog buns due to the combination of trans fat (0.28 g/100 g), saturated fat (5.56 g/100 g), and high sodium (593 mg/100 g), all of which are independently associated with elevated LDL cholesterol, endothelial dysfunction, and increased cardiovascular event risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.28 g/100 g), saturated fat (5.56 g/100 g), and sodium nitrates from processed cured sausage amplify inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid hot dog buns, particularly during flares, as the processed beef or chicken sausage contains trans fat, saturated fat, and sodium nitrates that are strongly pro-inflammatory; the arachidonic acid in red meat sausage further amplifies inflammatory eicosanoid production.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A hot dog bun is a popular Western-style fast food item consisting of a soft white bread roll filled with a grilled or steamed chicken or beef sausage and topped with meat sauce. In Singapore, hot dog buns are commonly sold at hawker stalls, school canteens, and convenience stores, and are frequently consumed as a breakfast or snack item. The bread bun is typically made from refined white flour, giving it a soft, slightly sweet texture with a tender crumb. The sausage is a processed, cured meat product — either chicken or beef — that contributes the majority of the protein, fat, sodium, and trans fat in the dish. The meat sauce topping adds additional flavour and sodium. Nutritionally, the hot dog bun provides 240 kcal per 100 g, with 12.17 g protein, 12.07 g fat (of which 5.56 g is saturated fat and 0.28 g is trans fat), and 20.68 g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at 593 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is approximately 72 (high), driven by the refined white bread bun, making this food unsuitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The trans fat content, while modest in absolute terms, is clinically significant as any amount of trans fat is associated with increased LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk. This food is not recommended for individuals with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is classified as halal when made with chicken or beef sausage from certified sources.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a hot dog bun?
Can diabetics eat hot dog buns?
How much protein in a hot dog bun?
Is hot dog bun high GI?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) retained from input at confidence 0.70 as indicative values. GI estimated from white bread analogue per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, which is the standard defensible approach for composite bread-based dishes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, and moisture data for this composite hot dog bun preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; this is consistent with a processed meat and refined bread product containing no significant vitamin C. The hot dog bun is a Western-origin food sold widely in Singapore; it does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as it is not a Singaporean-origin dish. Cuisine is classified as Western. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white bread (the dominant carbohydrate component) per University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 180 g serving = (72 × 20.68 × 1.80) / 100 = 26.7 g. Secondary micronutrients (calcium 44.44 mg, iron 1.39 mg, cholesterol 33.33 mg, saturated fat 5.56 g, trans fat 0.28 g) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery platform (grabfood), which is a forbidden primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; these values have been retained as indicative estimates consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for similar hot dog products (USDA SR Legacy item 21136 — frankfurter, beef) and are flagged at confidence 0.70 pending verification against an approved primary source.
